summary: Camille Fournier revisits her earlier critique of manager READMEs, arguing they remain ineffective tools for senior leaders. The core problem is that executives need to quickly understand what matters across many people with different communication styles, but standardized company tooling provides only lowest-common-denominator usefulness. READMEs are lazy and create a you-focused mindset that encourages information bubbles. Instead, she advocates for lightweight templates and ceremonies that show what you care about organizationally, while resisting the urge to processify everything. The key is driving clarity through selective standardization that benefits the collective, not just accommodating individual leader preferences.
key_points: Manager READMEs create a problematic you-focused mindset that encourages information bubbles and managing up to personality quirks
Standard company reporting (Jira, OKRs, dashboards) provides lowest-common-denominator usefulness that obscures as much as it reveals
The better alternative is lightweight templates and ceremonies for information sharing, discussion, and decision-making
Good templates show what information should be collected without dictating exactly how to collect it
Not everything should have a fixed process—some discussions need open-ended exploration rather than formal structure
money_quotes: READMEs are also rather lazy. There's a kernel of truth in their purpose: we want people to focus certain types of communication on what we believe is most valuable.
It creates a focus on you as the person to be managed up to. Your personality must be accommodated, your preferences honored.
I want to hear what I care about, yes, but I don't want to live in an information bubble either.
A big part of the job of senior/executive management is setting standards for doing certain types of work.
Dashboards are like alerts and tests in this way, they tend to catch what you know could go wrong, but rarely the surprise problems that lead to big incidents. Necessary, but insufficient.
Start simple and add on new details or processes only when it's clear you have a widespread gap.
Lead your organization by showing them where to spend their time and focus as a collective good, not just good for you.
key_insight: Senior leaders should drive clarity through selective lightweight templates and ceremonies that benefit the organization collectively, rather than manager READMEs that center individual preferences and create information bubbles.
